Community Education Kiosk

Learn About Colon Cancer Screening

Screening can help find colon cancer early—or even prevent it.

🎗 Learn About Colon Cancer Screening

Why Screening Matters

Most colorectal cancers start as a small growth called a polyp in the lining of the colon or rectum.

🎗 Learn About Colon Cancer Screening

Screening Tests

Several screening options are available. Below are three of the most commonly recommended options.

Flip the card to learn more.

🩺
Colonoscopy

A colonoscopy lets a healthcare provider look inside the entire colon and rectum. Polyps can often be removed during the same procedure.

It needs to be done once every 10 years if nothing abnormal is found.

Tap to flip back
💩
FIT

The fecal immunochemical test is an at-home stool test that looks for hidden blood.

It is usually done every year.

If the result is positive, a colonoscopy is needed.

Tap to flip back
🧪
FIT-DNA

The FIT-DNA test is an at-home stool test that looks for hidden blood and certain DNA changes.

It is usually done every 3 years.

If the result is positive, a colonoscopy is needed.

Tap to flip back

Want to learn more about colorectal cancer screening?

Scan the QR code to visit the American Cancer Society website.

🎗 Learn About Colon Cancer Screening

Who Should Be Screened?

Screening options depend on whether a person is at average risk or increased risk.

Flip the card to learn more.

🟦
Average Risk
Most adults ages 45–75 at average risk have several screening options.

You are generally at average risk if you:

  • Do not have a personal or family history of colorectal cancer.
  • Have never had advanced polyps.
  • Do not have inflammatory bowel disease or an inherited colorectal cancer syndrome.

Screening options: Colonoscopy, FIT, and FIT-DNA.

Tap to flip back
🟧
Increased Risk
Some people may need earlier or more frequent screening.

You may have increased risk if you have:

  • A personal history of colorectal cancer or advanced polyps.
  • A close family member (parent, brother, sister, or child) with colorectal cancer.
  • Inflammatory bowel disease.
  • An inherited colorectal cancer syndrome.

Colonoscopy is often the preferred test. People at increased risk may need to start screening earlier or be screened more often.

Tap to flip back
🎗 Learn About Colon Cancer Screening

Common Questions

Many people have questions or concerns about screening. Flip a card to learn more.

💰
Too expensive?

Most insurance plans cover recommended colorectal cancer screening.

If cost is still a concern, financial assistance programs may be available.

See the next page for more information.

Tap to flip back
⏰
Too busy?

A little time today can help prevent bigger problems tomorrow.

If you're busy, an at-home stool test may be a convenient screening option.

Tap to flip back
🚗
No ride?

Transportation shouldn't keep you from getting screened.

Call our Community Navigator to learn about transportation resources.

☎ (402) 559-5600

An at-home stool test may also be a convenient screening option.

Tap to flip back
😟
Afraid of finding something?

It's normal to feel worried.

Screening can prevent cancer or find it early, when treatment is more effective.

Need to talk? ☎ (402) 559-5600

Tap to flip back
🙂
Feel healthy?

Most colorectal cancers don't cause symptoms early.

That's why regular screening is recommended for adults ages 45–75—even if you feel healthy.

Tap to flip back
😕
Sounds uncomfortable?

Many people feel that way at first.

There are several screening options, including simple at-home stool tests.

Talk with your healthcare provider about which option is right for you.

Tap to flip back

Thank You for Learning About Colon Cancer Screening

The best screening test is the one that gets done.

Call your healthcare provider today to schedule your screening or request an at-home test kit.
